channel.title: SoundsBrown
channel.copyright: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International License.

item.title: Atari Punk Console
item.description: Atari Punk Console with CV mods. I also slapped vactrols on the two pots. References: - https://sdiy.info/wiki/Atari_Punk_Console - https://www.instructables.com/Voltage-Controlable-Atari-Punk-Console/

item.title: Power
item.description: Case contains an ATX power supply. Front panel breaks out the on/off switch, the standby and power on LEDs, and all the different positive voltages so it can be used like a bench supply. References: - https://www.electronics-tutorials.ws/blog/convert-atx-psu-to-bench-supply.html. - Power connectors. The backside contains bus boards that distribute +/-12V, +/-5V and GND using 5x2 pin headers and ribbon cables. TODO: Take a picture of the back.

item.title: Form factor
item.description: Eurorack “spec”: http://www.doepfer.de/a100_man/a100m_e.htm Ikea RAST = 19x10.5 inch = two rows of 3U modules ATX PSU Front panels from mandarin crates

item.title: Burning bootloaders
item.description: In Pololu config utility: Enable VCC output Set regulator mode to 5V Insert cable the right way around: In Arduino IDE: Board: Arduino Uno Programmer: Atmel STK500 Always use “Upload using programmer” Use first of two COM ports (e.g. COM9 if it advertises 9 and 10) Burning bootloader also just works

item.title: STM32
item.description: Note! The information below may be obsolete, check here: https://www.stm32duino.com/ https://github.com/stm32duino/wiki/wiki https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=337rDuCGeYs&list=WL&index=3&t=124s HW/SW setup Add to board manager URLs: http://dan.drown.org/stm32duino/package_STM32duino_index.json Also downloaded release from https://github.com/rogerclarkmelbourne/Arduino_STM32 Unzipped into C:\Users\Tom\Documents\Arduino\hardware\Arduino_STM32-1.0.0 Executed drivers/win/install_drivers.bat Jumpers on board are both set to 0 Pinout

item.title: Arduino IDE
item.description: Installing a dark theme Clone or download https://github.com/jeffThompson/DarkArduinoTheme Backup C:\Program Files (x86)\Arduino\lib\theme Overwrite with the theme directory from DarkArduinoTheme

item.title: Switch jack pinout
item.description: DPDT jacks View from bottom: There is a pin closest to the barrel which is not shown here and which can always go to GND. Thonkiconn

item.title: Optocouplers
item.description: Example circuits From the interwebs: Tested locally: 4N25 Test with circuit above suggests decay time of ~20ms after switching off. Input (J1) was square wave from LFO, output (J2) was to oscilloscope. Not great, but probably good enough for things like 1 PPS clock signals (200 BPM == 300 msec/beat). Note that https://hackaday.com/2018/05/09/optocouplers-defending-your-microcontroller-midi-and-a-hot-tip-for-speed/ suggests that adding some external circuitry can make the thing faster! H11L1 Didn’t want to play along – to be investigated.

item.title: Isolation
item.description: How do we make sure two interconnected modules don’t adversely affect one another? General Pass I/O connectors through diodes before they connect to the circuit. Use an opamp, see buffers. Digital signals Optocouplers. Dealing with differences in operating voltage Voltage dividers. Regulators.

item.title: Arduino
item.description: Arduino Nano pinout https://store.arduino.cc/arduino-nano Arduino MKR Zero pinout https://store.arduino.cc/arduino-mkr-zero-i2s-bus-sd-for-sound-music-digital-audio-data

item.title: Synth voltage conventions
item.description: Eurorack conventions Pitch: 1V/oct PSUs have +/-12V and +5V (not -5V) Measured results from my synths MicroBrute Pitch: 0V → 7.04V (seems slightly wobbly) Gate: 0.12V → 11.03V Env: 0V → 4.5V LFO: -5V → 5V KeyStep Pitch: accurate to within 1% of 1V/oct Gate: 0.03V → 5.18V Mod: 0V → 5.25V Clock: 3.45V square wave with 50% duty cycle DrumBrute Impact TBD (clock)

item.title: Power supplies and connectors
item.description: ATX Eurorack My power bus Voltage regulators

item.title: Github
item.description: Clone: git clone https://github.com/soundsbrown/brownnote.git Remember credentials: git config credential.helper store Add files: git add file1 file2 file3 See local changes: git diff –cached Commit changes: git commit -a -m “Message” Push changes: git push

item.title: Voltage dividers
item.description: If used with bipolar supply (e.g. +/- 12V), you divide the difference between + and -, and subtract that from + to get the output voltage: Vo = Vp - (Vp-Vn)*R1/(R1+R2) (where Vp is positive supply voltage, e.g. +12V and Vn is negative supply voltage, e.g. -12V)

item.title: Hugo
item.description: References https://gohugo.io/getting-started/quick-start/ https://therealmjp.github.io/posts/hello-hugo/ https://gohugo.io/hosting-and-deployment/hosting-on-github/ Site setup Install the EXTENDED VERSION to enable customization of the Hermit theme! Commands: hugo new site soundsbrown cd soundsbrown git init git submodule add https://github.com/Track3/hermit.git themes/hermit echo theme = “hermit” >> config.toml hugo new posts\hello.md hugo hugo server -D Some settings need to be copied from themes/hermit/exampleSite/config.toml to your own config.toml Structure and syntax Markdown cheat sheet: https://github.com/adam-p/markdown-here/wiki/Markdown-Cheatsheet

item.title: Op amps as buffers / voltage followers
item.description: Circuit Op amps Pinouts Op amp Pinout LM741 TL072TL082 TL074TL084LM324 Application notes Supply voltage is important: All tested amps max out at somewhere between 3V and 4V when using a 5V supply. Use 12V and put other components (e.g. Arduino) behind a regulator! When using unipolar (0-12V) supply: LM741 floors at 2V or so – anything below that gets clamped.
